subject: Old - Social Sc
sectionLabel: French Society During the Late Eighteenth Century
html:
Three estates of French society during 18th century:

  1. First Estate
  2. Second Estate
  3. Third Estate

Social Hierarchy

In 1774, Louis XVI of the Bourbon family was the king of France.

 French society was divided into three estates.

The clergy and nobility had special rights. The third estate formed the majority of the population and was an unprivileged class.

subject: Old - Social Sc
html:
Condition of French society which led to the outbreak of the revolution
​​​​​​​

Causes of French Revolution

On 5th May 1789 Louis XVI called together the meeting of the Estates General to impose a new tax.

Members of the Third Estate demanded one person should have one vote.

King (Louis XVI) rejected the demand (of one member-one vote) of the Third Estate.

On 20th June 1789, representatives of the third estate assembled in the hall of an indoor tennis court on the ground of Versailles and declared themselves as National Assembly. They demanded to curtail the power of the king.

King (Louis XVI) ordered troops to move into Paris which angered the natives and they attacked and destroyed the fort of Bastille on 14th July.

On 14th July 1789, the agitated crowd stormed the Fort of Bastille, a symbol of the tyranny of the old regime, and destroyed it.

  1. This triggered the chain of revolt across the country.
  2. Faced with the power of revolting subjects, Louis XVI accorded recognition to the National Assembly and agreed to give up his power.
  3. On 4th August 1789 all the feudal system of obligation and taxes were abolished by a decree.
  4. Churches' properties were confiscated and clergy too had to give up all their privileges.
  5. The National Assembly drafted the Constitution in 1791 and distributed the power among the legislature, executive, and judiciary along with the one-person-one-vote principle.

However, voting was restricted to Active citizens only 

Remaining men and women were classed as passive citizen who did not have any political right.

subject: Old - Social Sc
sectionLabel: France Abolishes Monarchy and Becomes a Republic
html:
Political clubs became a rallying point for discussions on governmental policies in which Jacobin club emerged as the favourite.

The members of the Jacobin club mainly belonged to the less prosperous sections of the society like- small shopkeepers, artisans, such as shoe-makers, watch-makers, and painters, as well as servants and daily wage workers.

Their leader was Maximilian Robespierre and they were called sans-culottes meaning without knee breeches.

On 10th August 1792, Jacobins planned an insurrection and imprisoned the royal family.

On 21st September 1792, it declared France a Republic.

Louis XVI was sentenced to death on the charge of Treason and was publicly executed on 21st January 1793.

The Jacobin leader Robespierre ruled ruthlessly from 1793 to 1794 and his reign was known as the Reign of Terror.

In July 1794 Robespierre was convicted for his excesses and executed by Guillotine.

The fall of Jacobin allowed the wealthier middle class to seize the power and ruled through an Executive, made up of five members, called DIRECTORY from 26th October 1795.

However, the frequent clash of Directors with legislative councils led to political instability. This paved the way for the rise of military dictatorship under Napoleon Bonaparte who ended the Directory in 1799 and became the 'First Council and later crowned himself as the Emperor of France in 1804.

subject: Old - Social Sc
chapter: HIS Socialism in Europe and the Russian Revolution
html:
The French Revolution opened up the possibility of creating a dramatic change in the structure of the society.

There were groups/ideas that looked to change society according to their thoughts and visions.

Liberals, Radicals and Conservatives were the three main groups and their main ideas were:

Conservatives Liberals Radicals
Opposed liberals and radicals. Tolerance of all religions Government based on majority.
Asserted that the past must be respected. Opposed the uncontrolled powers of the king. Opposed the privileges given to great landowners and wealthy factory owners.
Believed that the change should be slow. Supported the rights of the individuals Many of them also supported women's suffragette movements.
  Supported a representative, elected parliamentary government and independent judiciary. Supported private property but disliked concentration of property in the hands of a property in the hands of a few.
  Did not support universal adult franchise (right of every citizen including women)  
  Supported private property.  

Problems caused due to Industrial Revolution

  • Unemployment
  • Long working hours
  • Poor wages
  • Housing
  • Sanitation

SOCIALISM: Socialists were against private property but had differing ideas on how a society without property could operate. Some of the visions of socialists are as follows:

  1. Robert Owen: He sought to build a cooperative community.
  2. Louis Blanc: He wanted the government to encourage cooperatives and replace capitalist enterprises.
  3. Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels: They argued that the industrial society was 'capitalist'. The capitalists owned the capital invested in the factories, and the profit of capitalists was produced by workers. Thus, capitalists exploit the workers.
    Marx believed that this capitalist system would be triumphed by the workers and a socialist society would be established where all property would be socially controlled.

subject: Old - Social Sc
chapter: HIS Socialism in Europe and the Russian Revolution
html:
Autocracy: The rule by the king with absolute powers.

Bloody Sunday: Over 100 workers were killed and 300 wounded, when the procession of workers near Winter Palace was attacked by the police. This incident is known as Bloody Sunday. It started a series of events that became known as the 1905 Revolution.

Duma: The Russian Parliament.

The First World War (1914-1918): It was fought between two European alliances called -

  1. The Central Powers: Germany, Austria, and Turkey
  2. Allied Powers: France, Britain, and Russia.

subject: Old - Social Sc
chapter: HIS Socialism in Europe and the Russian Revolution
html:
February Revolution: In February 1917, there was an acute shortage of food in the workers' quarters. This led to many protests. Soldiers also joined the striking workers. Sensing trouble, the Tsar abdicated and a Provisional Government was formed. Thus, the monarch was brought down in the February Revolution of 1917.

April Theses: In April 1917, the Bolshevik leader Vladimir Lenin returned to Russia from his exile. He presented three demands which are known as Lenin's April Theses. These were:

  1. The war (First World War) must be brought to a close.
  2. Land must be transferred to the peasants.
  3. Banks must be nationalized.

The Revolution of October 1917 (also called the Bolshevik Revolution)

  • As the conflict between the Provisional Government and the Bolsheviks grew, Lenin emphasized on the uprising against the government as he feared the Provisional Government would set up a dictatorship.
  • He persuaded the Petrograd Soviet and the Bolshevik Party to agree to a socialist seizure of power.
  • .A Military Revolutionary Committee was appointed to organize the seizure.
  • The uprising began on 24 October.
  • By nightfall, the city was under the control of Bolsheviks and the ministers had surrendered.
  • Uprisings took place in other cities as well. This revolution is known as the October Revolution.

subject: Old - Social Sc
chapter: HIS Socialism in Europe and the Russian Revolution
html:
Changed after the Revolution of October 1917

Civil War: After the Revolution, entire Russia was divided into three groups-viz.

  1. The Bolsheviks (the reds),
  2. Socialist Revolutionaries (the greens) and
  3. The Tsarists (the 'whites')

A Civil War among these groups began in Russia. The greens and the whites were supported by France, USA, Britain, and Japan. Looting, banditry, and famine became common during the Civil War.

Collectivisation: Stalin started the Collectivisation programme in Russia. Under this, the peasants were forced to cultivate in collective farms (kolkhoz). The bulk of land and implements were transferred to the ownership of collective farms. Peasants worked on the land, and the Kolkhoz profit was shared.

chapter: HIS Nazism and the Rise of Hitler
html:
The First World War (1914-1918) - The First World War was fought between the Allied Powers and Central Powers.

MAIN COUNTRIES
The Allied Powers The Central Powers
France Germany
Britain Austria
Russia Turkey

The First World War ended with the defeat of Germany in 1918.

Treaty of Versailles (1919): After the end of the First World War, Germany had to sign a harsh and humiliating treaty at Versailles with the Allied powers.
Treaty of Versailles

  • Germany was held responsible for the war
  • Germany was forced to pay compensation amounting to 6 billion pounds under the War Guilt Clause
  • Germany lost all its overseas colonies, 10% of its population, 13% of its territories, 75% of its iron, and 26% of coal its to Allied Powers
  • Germany was demilitarized to weaken its powers
  • Resource-rich Rhineland was also occupied by the Allied powers

Weimer Republic: After the defeat of Germany in World War I and the abdication of the emperor, a democratic constitution with a federal structure was set up in Germany. But the Weimer Republic had to face many problems.

Problems Faced by Weimer Republic

subject: Old - Social Sc
chapter: HIS Nazism and the Rise of Hitler
html:
Rise and Fall of Hitler:

Reasons for the Rise of Hitler

  • Weakness of Weimer Republic
  • Nazi Propaganda
  • Unemployment
  • Harsh and humiliating provisions of the Treaty of Versailles
  • Great Recession of 1929-32
  • Fear of proletarianisation

subject: Old - Social Sc
chapter: HIS Nazism and the Rise of Hitler
html:
The Nazi Worldview

  • Racial supremacy:
    Hitler considered Nordic German Aryans at the top while Jews were located at the bottom.
  • Concept of Lebensraum:
    It means living space. Hitler believed that new territories had to be acquired for the settlement of the German people
  • The Nazis believed in strong leadership. Hitler adopted the title
    Der Fuhrer meaning 'the leader'. Slogans like 'Adolf Hitler is Germany, Germany is Adolf Hitler' was common.
  • National Socialism:
    The Nazis criticized both capitalism and socialism out they espoused a philosophy called National Socialism.

subject: Old - Social Sc
chapter: HIS Nazism and the Rise of Hitler
html:
Youth in Nazi Germany:
Hitler felt that a strong Nazi society could be established only by teaching children Nazi ideology. He took the following steps to achieve this:

  1. Jews were dismissed from the schools.
  2. Children were segregated. Germans and Jews could not sit together. Subsequently, 'undesirable children'- Jews, the physically handicapped, Gypsies - were thrown out of schools.
  3. School textbooks were rewritten. Racial science was introduced to justify Nazi ideas of race.
  4. Children were taught to be loyal and submissive, hate Jews and worship Hitler.
  5. Youth organizations were made responsible for educating German youth in the 'spirit of National Socialism'.
  6. After a period of rigorous ideological and physical training, they joined the Labour Service. Then they had to serve in the armed forces and enter one of the Nazi organizations.

Women in Nazi Germany:

  1. Girls were taught to become good mothers and rear pureblooded Aryan children.
  2. Girls had to maintain the purity of the race, distance themselves from Jews, look after the home, and teach their children Nazi ideology.
  3. Hitler said: "In my state, the mother is the most important citizen." But in Nazi Germany, all mothers were not treated equally.
  4. Women who bore racially undesirable children were punished and those who produced racially desirable children were awarded.
  5. Women were encouraged to produce many children.
  6. All 'Aryan' women who deviated from the prescribed code of conduct were publicly condemned, and severely punished.

chapter: HIS Forest Society and Colonialism (Internally Assessed)
html:
Due to deforestation, diversity in areas of natural vegetation and wildlife is fast disappearing.

Between 1700 and 1995, the period of industrialization, 13.9 million sqkm of forest or 9.3 per cent of the world's total area was cleared for

  • Industrial Uses
  • Cultivation
  • Pastures
  • Fuel Wood

Causes of large-scale deforestation during colonial rule

  • Expansion of cultivation for food grains
  • Growing need for tea, coffee, and rubber plantations
  • For Imperial shipbuilding
  • Railways
  • Expansion of the production of Commercial crops
  • To enhance the state revenue by converting forest land into agricultural land

Dietrich Brandis suggested the following steps to manage the forests:

  • People had to be trained in the science of conservation.
  • Rules had to be framed about the use of forest resources.
  • Felling of trees and grazing had to be restricted.

subject: Old - Social Sc
chapter: HIS Forest Society and Colonialism (Internally Assessed)
html:
Examples of the things that come from forest:

The British decided to invite a German expert, Dietrich Brandis, for advice, to manage and control the use of Forests by local people and the reckless cutting of trees by traders and made him the first Inspector General of Forests in India.

Brandis set up the Indian Forest Service in 1864 and he helped in formulating the Indian Forest Act of 1865.

Type of Forests -
The 1878 Act divided forests into three categories: reserved, protected, and village forests.

(i) Reserved Forest Best Forest Villages could not take anything
(ii) Protected Forest Good Forest Villages could take resource with permission
(iii) Village Forest Average & Below Average Villages could take resource with permission

The Imperial Forest Research Institute was set up at Dehradun in 1906. The system they taught there was called scientific forestry.

In the scientific forestry system, natural forests which had lots of different types of trees were cut down. In their place, one type of tree was planted in straight rows. This is also called the plantation.

 Different Names of the Shifting cultivation or Swidden agriculture

Sl. NO. Region Name of Shifting Agriculture
1 South-East Asia Lading
2 Central America Milpa
3 Africa Chitemene or Tavy
4 Sri Lanka Chena
5 India Dhya, Penda, Bewar, Nevad, Jhum, Podu, Khandad and Kumri

Impact of Forest Law

  • After the implementation of the act, almost all the everyday practices of natives became illegal like Cutting wood for their houses, Hunting and Fishing, Grazing their cattle, collecting honey, Collecting fruits and roots, Collection of Mahua and other forest products.
  • It was also common for police constables and forest guards to harass people by demanding free food from them.
  • Women who collected fuelwood were especially worried for their safety.
  • People were now forced to steal wood from the forests, and if they were caught, they were at the mercy of the forest guards who would take bribes from them.
  • Many pastoralist and nomadic communities like the Korava, Karacha, and Yerukula of the Madras
    Presidency lost their livelihoods, and some of them began to be called criminal tribes.
  • They were forced to work instead in factories, mines, and plantations, under government supervision.

New Trades and Employments: After the new forest laws many communities left their traditional occupation and started trading in Forest products: (Some examples)

  • Mundurucu people of the Brazilian Amazon, began to collect latex from wild rubber trees
  • Banjaras (nomadic communities) of India traded elephant and other goods
  • Korava, Karacha and Yerukula of the Madras, began to work in factories, mines, and plantations

Steps of shifting cultivation:

  1. Parts of the forest are cut and burnt in rotation.
  2. Seeds are sown in the ashes after the first monsoon rains, and the crop is harvested by October-November.
  3. Such plots are cultivated for a couple of years
  4. Then left fa Now for 12 to 18 years for the forest to grow back.
  5.  Then they move to the next part of the forest.

Hunting practice was prohibited by the Forest laws for the forest dwellers. But under colonial rule, the scale of hunting increased.

  1. The British saw large animals as signs of a wild, primitive, and savage society. They believed that by killing dangerous animals the British would civilize India.
  2. Over 80,000 tigers, 150,000 leopards and 200,000 wolves were killed for reward in the period 1875-1925.
  3. A British administrator, George Yule, killed 400 tigers. Initially, certain areas of forests were reserved for hunting.

subject: Old - Social Sc
chapter: HIS Forest Society and Colonialism (Internally Assessed)
html:
Rebellion in the Forest:

Revolt Leader Region
Santhal Ravolt (1855-56) Siddhu and Kanu Santhai Pargana(Jharkhand)
Munda Revolt (1898-1899) Birsa Munda Chhotanagpur (Jharkhand)
Rampa Revolt (1879 - 99) Alluri Sitaram Raju Andhra Pradesh

People of Bastar
Bastar is located in the southernmost part of Chhattisgarh and Borders Andhra Pradesh, Orissa and Maharashtra.

  1. A number of different communities live in Bastar such as Maria and Muria Gonds, Dhurwas, Bhatras and Halbas.
  2. They speak different languages but share common customs and beliefs.
  3.  The river Indrawati flows across Bastar east to west.

Some villages were allowed to stay on in reserved forests on the condition that they worked free for the forest department in cutting and transporting trees and protecting the forests from fires. These villages were called Forest villages or Van Gram.

One of the important figures of this forest revolt was Gunda Dhur.

Though the revolt was suppressed, in a major victory for the rebels,

  1. Work on reservation was temporarily suspended.
  2. The area to be reserved was reduced to roughly half of that planned before 1910.

Reason for the Bastar Revolt

  • In 1905, the colonial government proposed:
    • to reserve two-thirds of the Forest.
    • stop shifting cultivation
    • stop Hunting
    • stop the collection of Forest produce
  • People of other villages were displaced without any notice or compensation
  • Villagers were suffering from increased land rents
  • Demands for free labours and goods by colonial official

In 1910 through mango boughs, a lump of earth, chillies, and arrows the villagers started spreading messages for the rebellion against the British.

During the revolt, Bazaars (Markets) were looted, houses of officials and traders, schools, and police stations were burnt, robbed, and attacked.

The Adivasi leaders tried to negotiate but the British troops fired upon them. It took three months for the British to regain control.

In a major victory for the rebels

  1. Work on the forest reservation was temporarily suspended.
  2. The area to be reduced to roughly half of what was planned before 1910

subject: Old - Social Sc
chapter: HIS Forest Society and Colonialism (Internally Assessed)
html:
Java is now famous as a rice-producing island in Indonesia.

  1. The Kalangs of Java were a community of skilled forest cutters and shifting cultivators.
  2. The Dutch first imposed rents on land being cultivated in the forest and then exempted some villages from paying it if they work collectively to provide free labour and buffalos for cutting and transporting timber. This system was known as Blandongdiensten.

The Dutch enacted forest laws in Java which:

  1. Restricted villagers' access to the forests.
  2. Made it mandatory that wood would only be cut for specified purposes like making river boats or constructing houses.
  3. These woods could be cut only from specific forests under close supervision.
  4. Penalized the villagers for grazing cattle in young stands, transporting wood without a permit, or travelling on forest roads with horse carts or cattle.

The Samins protested this advancement of Dutch

  1. They were led by Surontiko Samin who argued that the state had not created the wind, water, earth and wood, so it could not own it and any demand for taxes.
  2. They protested by
    1. Lying down on their land when the Dutch came to survey it.
    2. By refusing to pay taxes or fines.
    3. They also refused to perform free labour.

In Java, just before the Japanese occupied the region, the Dutch followed a scorched earth policy, destroying sawmills, and burning huge piles of giant teak logs so that they would not fall into Japanese hands. The Japanese then exploited the forests recklessly for their own war industries, forcing forest villagers to cut down forests.

In India, from Mizoram to Kerala, dense forests have survived only because villages protected them in sacred groves known as sarnas, devarakudu, kan, rai, etc.

Nomadic pastoralists - People who depend on cattle for their livelihood and move from place to place in search of good pastures for them.

Bhabar is an area of dry forest below the foothills of Garhwal and Kumaun region.

The vast meadows in the high mountains are called Bugyal.

Kharif is an autumn crop, usually harvested between September and October.

Rabi is the spring crop, usually harvested after March.

Reason for movement Advantages Exchange items
They don't have regular field to get food for the whole year. Gives sufficient time for natural restoration of vegetation. Meat, milk, wool, animal skin, and other native products,
In search of pasture and water for their animals. It allows manure to the field.

To protect their livestock from harsh weather. The seasonal interaction between two different communities ensures coexistence.

subject: Old - Social Sc
chapter: HIS Pastoralists in the Modern World (PA Only)
html:
Effects of Colonial Rule on the lives of pastoralists

  • Available areas of pastureland declined and disappeared.
  • Their movements got restricted.
  • Intensive grazing on available pastures.
  • Quality of pastures declined.
  • Deterioration of animal stock.
  • Underfed cattle died in large numbers.

Coping with the changes:

  1. They reduced the number of cattle in their herd.
  2. Changed their direction of movement.
  3. Some began buying land and settling down.
  4. Some took to more extensive trading.
  5. Some lost their livestock and became labourers.

subject: Old - Social Sc
chapter: HIS Pastoralists in the Modern World (PA Only)
html:
Policies regarding forests in India and Africa during the colonial period:

S. No. Colonial Policy in the Forest of India  Colonial Policy in the Forests of Africa
1. Conversion of pastures into agricultural and by encouraging local farmers to convert pastures into agricultural land to raise land taxes. The conversion of pastures into agricultural land by the British colonial government by encouraging local farmers to convert pastures into agricultural land in East Africa.
2. Ban on the movement of shepherds by categorizing forests by forest laws. The colonial rule occupied about 60 percent of the grazing area of the Maasai and stopped their movement.
3. In 1871, the colonial government passed the Criminal Tribes Act, declaring many communities of pastoralists as natural and born criminals. Restricting the movement of pastoralists by converting as many as 8 pastures into hunting grounds, such as the Maasai Mara and Samburu National Parks in Kenya and the Serengeti Park in Tanzania.
4. In the nineteenth century, a tax per cattle was collected by levying a pastoral tax. The chiefs of the Maasai sub-groups were appointed by the colonial government.

In Africa, Maasai lost 60% of their pre-colonial land at the hands of Whites.

  1. They were forbidden to enter into White locality
  2. Their restrictions and confinement to semi-arid areas with frequent droughts made their life difficult and they lost half of their livestock within two years 1933-1934.
  3. Large areas of grazing land were also turned into game reserves like Massai Mara and Samburu National Park in Kenya and Serengeti Park in Tanzania.
  4. In 1885 Massailand was cut into half with an international boundary between British Kenya and German Tanganyika.
  5. In 1919 Tanganyika came under British rule after the defeat of Germany in World War I.
  6. It attended independence in 1961 and united with Zanzibar to form Tanzania in 1964.

Maasai society was divided into two social groups: Elders and Warriors.

  1. Elders formed the ruling group who met in periodic councils to
    1. Decide affairs of the community and
    2. Settle disputes.
  2. The warriors consisted of young people who defended the community and were mainly responsible for
    1. The protection of the tribe and
    2. Organizing cattle raids which were considered a wealth.

The Britishers imposed restrictions on raiding and warfare and even appointed chiefs of different sub-groups of Maasai who were made responsible for the affairs of the group.

Due to the interference of the colonial government in the social system of Maasai change occur at two levels:

  1. The traditional difference based on age, between the elders and warriors, was disturbed.
  2. A new distinction between wealthy and poor pastoralists got developed.

html:
Physical Features of India:

Young fold mountains stretch over the northern borders of India.

Himalayan Mountains: Total length: 2400 Kms. Width: 400 km in Kashmir to 150 Km in Arunachal Pradesh

Great or Inner Himalayas or the Himadri: It is the northern most range of the Himalayas. It is the most continuous range and contains all the prominent Himalayan peaks. (Average height-6,000 metres). Some highest peaks include- Mount Everest, Kanchenjunga, Makalu, Nanga Parbat etc.

Himachal (lesser Himalaya): The altitude varies between 3,700 and 4,500 metres. Pir Panjal, Dhaula Dhar and Mahabharat are important ranges. Famous valley of Kashmir Kangra and Kullu lies here.

Shiwaliks: This is the outermost range of the Himalayas. The altitude varies between 900 and 1100 metres. The longitudinal valley lying between the lesser Himalayas and the Shiwaliks is known as Duns. Dehra Dun, Kotli Dun and Patli Dun are some of the well-known Duns.

The Himalayas consists of three parallel ranges in its longitudinal extent. These mountain ranges run in a west-east direction from the Indus to the Brahmaputra.

  • The northern-most range is known as the Great or Inner Himalayas or the Himadri. 
  • The range lying to the south of the Himadri forms the most rugged mountain system and is known as Himachal or lesser Himalaya.
  • The outer-most range of the Himalayas is called the Shiwaliks.

The Himalayas have been divided on the basis of regions from west to east. These divisions are demarcated by river valleys. These are:

  1. Punjab Himalayas: between Indus and Satluj rivers.
  2. Kumaon Himalayas: between Satluj and Kali rivers.
  3. Nepal Himalayas: between Kali and Tista rivers.
  4. Assam Himalayas: between Tista and Dihang rivers.
  5. Purvachal Himalays: Extended along the eastern border of India.

subject: Old - Social Sc
html:
The Northern Plain has been formed by the interplay of the three major river systems, namely- the Indus, the Ganga and the Brahmaputra along with their tributaries.

The Northern plain is formed of alluvial soil. It is agriculturally a very productive part of India.

The Northern Plain is broadly divided into three sections:

  1. Punjab Plains: This is the western part of the Northern Plain. It is formed by the Indus and its tributaries-the Jhelum, the Chenab, the Ravi, the Beas and the Satluj. This section of the plain is dominated by the doabs (do means two and ab means water)
  2. The Ganga Plain: It extends between Ghaggar and Teesta rivers. It is spread over the states of Haryana, Delhi, U.P., Bihar, Jharkhand and West Bengal.
  3. The Brahmputra Plain: This plain is formed by the Brahmputra and its tributaries. It lies mainly in the state of Assam.

The Northern Plain can be divided into four regions according to the variations in relief features:

  1. Bhabar: This region lies parallel to the slopes of the Shiwaliks. The rivers, after descending from the mountains deposit pebbles in this region. All the streams disappear in this bhabar belt.
  2. Terai: It lies to the south of bhabar. The streams and rivers reemerge here. It is a wet, swampy, and marshy region.
  3. Bhangar: Bhangar is the largest part of the northern plain and is formed of the oldest alluvial soil. They lie above the flood plains of the rivers and present a terrace like feature. The soil in this region contains calcareous deposits locally known as kankar.
  4. Khadar: The floodplains formed by newer and younger deposits are called khadar. They are renewed almost every year and so are fertile.

subject: Old - Social Sc

The Peninsular Plateau consists of two broad divisions, namely the Central Highlands and the Deccan Plateau.

The black soil area of the peninsular plateau is known as Deccan Trap. This is of volcanic origin and hence the rocks are igneous.

1. The Indian Desert lies towards the western margins of the Aravali Hills.

The western coast:

  2. It is relatively narrow.
  3. It consists of three sections. The northern part of the coast is called the Konkan, the central stretch is called the Kannad Plain, while the southern stretch is referred to as the Malabar Coast.

The eastern coast:

  2. This plain is wide and level.

  4. Large rivers such as the Mahanadi, the Godavari, the Krishna, and the Kaveri have formed extensive delta on this coast.

The Chilika Lake is the largest salt water lake in India. It is located in the state of Odisha.

The Western Ghats and the Eastern Ghats mark the western and eastern edges of the Deccan Plateau respectively.
 

Western Ghats Eastern Ghats
The Western Ghats lie along the Arabian Sea The Eastern Ghats lie along the Bay of Bengal.
They are continuous and can be crossed through passes only. They are discontinuous and irregular and dissected by rivers.

html:
The term Drainage describes the river system of a particular area.

The area drained by a river system is called a Drainage Basin.

The world's largest drainage basin is the Amazon River.

The Ganga River is the largest river basin in India.

Any elevated area like mountain or an upland that separates two drainage basins is called a water divide.

Indian Rivers are divided into two major groups:

  1. The Himalayan Rivers
    1. The Indus River system
    2. The Ganga River system
    3. The Brahmaputra River system
  2. The Peninsular Rivers
    1. The Narmada Basin
    2. The Tapi Basin
    3. The Godavari Basin
    4. The Mahanadi Basin
    5. The Krishna Basin
    6. The Kaveri Basin

Himalayas Rivers

  • In its upper course the speed of water is very high so it cuts the valley to form waterfalls, V-shaped valleys, rapids, gorges, and canyons, and carries huge loads of silt with them.
  • In their middle course vertical erosion gives way to side erosion and hence forms alluvial fans, alluvial plains meander, etc.
  • In its lower course rivers loses its carrying capacity due to the absence of slope and hence deposit silt and debris to form flood plain, braided channels, ox-bow lakes, delta etc.

Difference between the Himalayan and Peninsular River System:

Himalayan River System Peninsular River System
They are perennial (water throughout the year) as they are fed by the melting snow. They are seasonal as they are fed on rain-water.
They have a long course. They have shorter course.
They are mostly flown through unstable areas of the new fold mountains of Himalaya. They are mostly flown through stable areas of Gondwana land.
They perform high erosion and depositional work in their course. They don't perform these activities on such scale.

The headwater of the Ganga, called Bhagirathi, is joined by Alaknanda River at Devaprayag in Uttarakhand.

At Haridwar the Ganga emerges from the mountains on to the plains.

Brahmaputra River is known as Tsang Po in Tibet and Jamuna in Bangladesh.

The Brahmaputra River enters India in Arunachal Pradesh where it is called Dihang.

Sunderban Delta is the world's largest and fastest-growing delta.

In peninsular India, west-flowing rivers are Narmada and Tapti which forms Estuaries instead of delta.

Narmada River originates from Amarkantak Hills in Madhya Pradesh and form Dhuadhar waterfall near Jabalpur (M.P.).

River Kaveri makes the second biggest waterfall in India called Shivsamudram.

India's biggest waterfall is Jog waterfall on Sharavati River in Karnataka.

subject: Old - Social Sc
html:
The atmospheric conditions, landforms and drainage are the three basic elements of any natural environment.

Weather refers to the state of the atmosphere over an area at any point of time.

Climate refers to the sum total of weather conditions and variations over a large area for a long period of time (more than thirty years).

The elements of weather and climate are the same, i.e. temperature, atmospheric pressure, wind, humidity, and precipitation.

The weather conditions fluctuate very often even within a day. But there is some common pattern over a few weeks or months, i.e. days are cool or hot, windy or calm, cloudy or bright, and wet or dry.

On the basis of the generalized monthly atmospheric conditions, the year is divided into seasons such as winter, summer and rainy seasons.

subject: Old - Social Sc
html:
Factors Affecting the Climate of India

  • Latitude
  • Altitude
  • Distance from sea
  • Ocean
  • Relief features or landscape or surface of the earth
  • Pressure and Wind system
Sl no. Factors affecting climate of India How do they affect climate Reason
1 Latitude The more we move away from equator, the colder will be the climate. Due to the curvature of the earth, the amount of solar energy decreases as we move from the equator to the poles
2 Altitude As we move up in height colder will be the climate. As the atmosphere becomes less dense and temperature decreases with increasing height
3 Distance from sea The more a place is at distance from sea the more extreme climatic condition it will have. The moderating influence of sea (sea breeze) decreases with distance.
4 Ocean current The climatic condition of a coastal place is affected by the warm and cold current flowing past by As cold or warm current accordingly modify the nature of onshore winds and affects the climate.
5 Relief features or landscape or surface of earth. Landscape like high mountain acts as barrier for cold or hot winds, they also cause precipitation or rain shadow on leeward. Due to their size and height they can restrict the flow of winds and alter the climate of a place.
6 Pressure and Wind system They are dependent on the latitude and altitude of a place and hence has an effect on climate accordingly. Air pressure decreases with increasing height. Winds blow from high air pressure areas to low air pressure areas.

The Tropic of Cancer passes through the middle of the country from the Rann of Kuchchh (Gujarat) in the west to Mizoram in the east.

Thus India's climate has characteristics of both tropical as well as subtropical climates.

The Himalayas prevent the cold winds from Central Asia from entering the subcontinent. Therefore, we experience comparatively milder winters as compared to central Asia.

Atmospheric Condition associated with Climate and Weather

  • Pressure and Surface winds
  • Upper Air Circulation
  • Western cyclonic Disturbance
  • Tropical cyclones

India lies in the region of north easterly winds. These winds originate from the subtropical high-pressure belt of the northern hemisphere.

Due to the rotation of the earth winds deflect towards the right in the northern hemisphere and towards the left in the southern hemisphere by a force called the Coriolis force. This is also known as ‘Ferrel’s law.

subject: Old - Social Sc
html:
Four Main Seasons

  • The Cold Weather Season
  • The hot weather season
  • The advancing monsoon
  • The retreating monsoon (with some regional variations)

The cold weather season (Winter Season)

  • It begins from mid-November in northern India and stays till February.
  • The temperature decreases from the south to the north.
  • Days are warm and nights are cold. Frost is common in the north and the higher slopes of the Himalayas experience snowfall.
  • The northeast trade winds prevail over the country.
  • In northern India, a feeble high-pressure region develops, with light winds moving outwards from this area.

The Hot Weather Season (The Summer Season):

  1. It begins from March to May.
  2. The summer months experience rising temperatures and falling air pressure in the northern part of the country.
  3. A striking feature of the hot weather season is the Loo. Loo is strong, gusty, hot, dry winds blowing during the day over north and northwestern India.
  4. Dust storms are common and sometime may bring light rain and cool breeze.
  5. During summer Sometimes localized thunderstorm along with violent wind, torrential downpours accompanied by hail occurs which is called Kaal Baisaki in West Bengal.
  6. Pre-monsoon showers are common especially, in Kerala and Karnataka. They help in the early ripening of mangoes, and are often referred to as mango showers.

Advancing Monsoon (The Rainy Season):

  1. The duration of the monsoon is between 100-120 days from early June to mid-September.
  2. By early June the low-pressure condition over the northern plains intensified.
  3. It attracts the trade winds of the southern hemisphere.
  4. These trade winds originate over warm subtropical areas of the southern ocean and enter India as South-west Monsoon.
  5. As these winds blow over the warm oceans, they bring abundant moisture to the sub-continent and do precipitation.
  6. The maximum rainfall of this season is received in the northeastern part of the country. Mawsynram in the southern ranges of the Khasi Hills receives the highest average rainfall in the world.
  7. When the axis of the monsoon trough lies over the plains, rainfall is good in these parts.

Retreating Monsoon (The Autumn Season):

  1. During October-November the low pressure over northern plains(due to heat) become gradually weak and is replaced by a high-pressure system (due to the cooling effect of rain during monsoon).
  2. The southwest monsoon winds weaken and gradually withdraw from the northern plain.
  3. This retreat is marked by clear skies and a rise in temperature with moist land.
  4. Owing to this condition of high temperature and humidity the weather became oppressive during the day. This is commonly known as October Heat.
  5. Now low-pressure conditions get transferred to the Bay of Bengal and hence cause Cyclones to the Eastern Coast of India.
    • Monsoon is called the unifying bond of the Indian Sub-Continent
    • In fact India's fate rests on Monsoon.

html:
On the basis of its residence it is of two types-

  1. Endemic: which are purely native
  2. Exotic: which are brought from some other place long ago

The term Flora is used to denote plants of a particular region or period.

Importance of Forests

  • Instrumental in bringing rain
  • Gives medicines
  • Supplies oxygen
  • Supplies manure
  • Controls floods and land degradation
  • Natural Habitat for the wildlife
  • Maintains the climate of a place
  • Supplies food stock
  • Prevents soil erosion and maintains its fertility
  • Controls the temperature of a place
  • Provides fuel

Tropical Evergreen Forest (Tropical rain forest)

  • Annual Rainfall: >200 cm
  • Important Vegetation: Ebony, mahogany, rosewood, rubber, cinchona
  • Important wildlife: Elephant, monkey, lemur, deer, variety of birds, sloth, scorpions, snakes
  • Found in States: Western Ghats, Andaman & Nicobar Islands, Lakshadweep, Assam (upper part), Tamil Nadu (coast)

Tropical Evergreen Forest (Tropical rain forest)
Characteristics:

  1. Warm and wet climate
  2. Trees shed leaves at different times of the year
  3. Height of tree more than 60 m
  4. Trees are very dense and form canopy
  5. Woods they provide are hard and durable

Tropical Deciduous Forest (Monsoon forest) (Wet deciduous)

  • Annual Rainfall: 100 - 200 cm
  • Important Vegetation: Teak, sal, shisham, bamboo, khair, sandalwood, arjun, mulberry
  • Important Wildlife: Lion, tiger, deer, pig, elephant, variety of birds, lizards, snakes, tortoise
  • Found in States: Jharkhand, Orissa, Chhattisgarh, North-Eastern states

Tropical Deciduous Forest (Monsoon forest)
Characteristics

  • It is the most widespread forest in India.
  • Trees are the same as tropic evergreen forests, Teak being the dominant one.
  • Trees shed their leaves for about six to eight weeks in dry summer every year.
  • Woods are moderately hard.

Tropical Deciduous Forest (Monsoon forest) (Dry deciduous)

  • Annual Rainfall: 70 - 100 cm
  • Important Vegetation: Teak, sal, peepal, neem
  • Important Wildlife: Lion, tiger, deer, pig, elephant, variety of birds, lizards, snakes, tortoise
  • Found in States: Bihar, Uttar Pradesh

Thorn Forest & Bushes (Scrubs)

  • Annual Rainfall: <70 cm
  • Important Vegetation: Acacia, palm, cacti, euphorbias
  • Important Wildlife: Rats, mice, rabbits, foxes, wolves, wild ass, camel
  • Found in States: Gujarat, Rajasthan, Arid area of Madhya Pradesh, Haryana Chhattisgarh

Thorn Forest & Bushes
Characteristics

  • Mostly thorny bushes and trees
  • Trees are deep-rooted.
  • Leaves modifies into spine & stem have a waxy coating to reduce loss of water

Mangrove Forest (Tidal forest)

  • Important vegetation: Sundari, Palm, Coconut, Agar, Keora
  • Important Wildlife: Bengal tiger, turtles, crocodiles, gharials, snakes
  • Found in States: West Bengal (mostly deltaic region of Mahanadi, Krishna, Kaveri)
  • Characteristics: Sundari trees have root submerged in saline water. These provide durable hard timber.

html:
Species of animals are referred as Fauna.

The system of interaction among biotic component and their interaction with the abiotic components of a particular area is called Ecosystem. Eg. Pond ecosystem, river ecosystem, lake, forest, grassland, etc.
Ecosystem

  1. Biotic component
    • Producer i.e. plants
    • Consumer i.e. humans, animals
    • Decomposer i.e. bacteria, fungi
  2. Abiotic component
    • Climatic factors like rain, wind, temperature
    • Soil, minerals, etc.

Ecosystem

  1. Terrestrial (land)
    • Forest
    • Grassland
    • Desert
  2. Aquatic (water)
    • Pond
    • Lake
    • River
    • Estuary

A very large ecosystem of an area having distinct types of flora and fauna is called Biome.
Relation between different systems:

  • Biosphere
  • Biome
  • Ecosystem
  • Community
  • Population
  • Individual

Wildlife Protection Act was implemented in 1972 in India.

India is the only country in the world that has both Lion and Tiger.

Gir forest of Gujarat is the last remaining habitat of Asiatic lion.

The country has around 90,000 animal species, 2,000 species of birds, 2,546 species of fishes, and 5-8% of the world's amphibians, reptiles, and mammals.

To protect the flora and fauna the government has taken the following steps:

  1. Eighteen biosphere reserves have been set up in the country to protect flora and fauna.
  2. Financial and technical assistance is provided to many
  3. Project Tiger, Project Rhino, Project Great Indian Bustard, and many other eco-developmental projects have been introduced
  4. 103 National Parks, 535 Wildlife sanctuaries and Zoological gardens are set up to take care of the natural habitat of the fauna. Botanical Gardens by the government since 1992.

subject: Old - Social Sc
html:
The people are important to develop the economy and society.

In India, the first census was held in the year 1872. But the first complete census however was taken in the year 1881. Since then censuses have been held regularly every tenth year (except in 2021 due to Covid-19).

India's population as on March 2011 stood at 121 crore (presently more than 130 crore).

India's population accounts for 17.5 per cent of the world population.

Most populous state in India: Uttar Pradesh

Least populous state in India: Sikkim

Population density of India: 382 persons per square Km
 

State with highest population density: Bihar (1102 persons per square Km)

State with lowest population density: Arunachal Pradesh (17 only)

Only Bangladesh and Japan have higher average population densities than India.

Census 2011 is the 15th national census of the country since 1872.

The population of India at 1210.2 million (121 crore) is almost equal to the combined population of USA, Indonesia, Brazil, Pakistan, Bangladesh and Japan.

subject: Old - Social Sc
sectionLabel: Population Growth and Process of Population Change
html:
Processes of population change:
There are three main processes of change of population:

  1. Birth rates: number of live births per thousand persons in a year.
  2. Death rates: number of deaths per thousand persons in a year.
  3. Migration: movement of people across regions and territories. It is of two types
    1. Migration can be internal (within the country)
    2. Migration can be international (between the countries).

Migration plays a very significant role in changing the composition and distribution of population.

The main cause of the rate of growth of the Indian population has been the rapid decline in death rates.

Internal migration does not change the size of the population, but influences the distribution of the population within the nation.

Since 1981, birth rates have also started declining gradually, resulting in a gradual decline in the rate of population growth.

Mostly migrations have been from rural to urban areas because of the push factor e.g. adverse conditions of poverty and unemployment in rural areas and the pull factor e.g. increased employment opportunities and better living conditions in urban areas.

In India, the rural-urban migration has resulted in a steady increase in the percentage of population in cities and towns. These are:

Years Percentage Million Plus Cities
2001 27.78 35
2011 31.80 53

The urban population has increased from 17.29 percent of the total population in 1951 to 31.80 percent in 2011.

Age composition is one of the most basic characteristics of a population. The population of a nation is generally grouped into three broad categories:

  1. Children (below 15 years): They are economically unproductive and need to be provided with food, clothing, education, and medical care.
  2. Working Age (15-59): They are economically productive and biologically reproductive. They comprise the working population.
  3. Aged (59 years): They can be economically productive though they may have retired and they may be working voluntarily.

Sex ratio is defined as the number of females per 1000 males in the population. It is an important social indicator to measure the extent of equality between males and females in a society.

The Sex ratio in India is 940.

The state having highest sex ratio: Kerala (1084)

The State having lowest sex ratio: Haryana (977)

Literacy rates: A person aged 7 years and above who can read and write with understanding in any language, is treated as literate.

Occupational Structure: The distribution of the population according to different types of occupation is referred to as the occupational structure. Occupations are generally classified as primary, secondary, and tertiary. These are:

  1. Primary activities include agriculture, animal husbandry, forestry, fishing, mining and quarrying etc.
  2. Secondary activities include manufacturing industry, building, and construction work etc.
  3. Tertiary activities include transport, communications, commerce, administration and other services.

Government of India initiated the comprehensive Family Planning Programme in 1952 to improve individual health and welfare.

The NPP 2000 provides a policy framework for imparting free and compulsory school education up to 14 years of age, reducing infant mortality rate to below 30 per 1000 live births, achieving universal immunization of children against all vaccine-preventable diseases, promoting delayed marriage for girls, and making family welfare a people-centered programme.

The population growth rate of Chandigarh, Uttarakhand and Assam is around or equal to India's population growth rate (17 %).
